signature.instructions:
Task: Clean and categorize name strings into canonical buckets, producing a single structured JSON result.

Inputs
- entity_schema: one of "LegalEntity", "Organization", or "Person". If an unexpected schema is provided (e.g., "Company"), treat it using the Organization/LegalEntity rules.
- strings: an array of raw strings. Each string may be a single name, a variant, or a list of multiple names/variants.

Output
- Return a single JSON object with exactly these keys in this order:
  - name: []
  - alias: []
  - weakAlias: []
  - previousName: []
- Each value is an array of unique, cleaned strings. Include empty arrays when no values exist.
- Do not include any additional text or fields in the output.

General principles
- Do not invent, expand, or infer names not present in the input (e.g., do not expand acronyms like VTB).
- Do not generate extra variants (e.g., do not drop or add legal suffixes; do not abbreviate or expand).
- Preserve original casing of the cleaned input (no auto title-casing or lowercasing).
- Do not correct typos (e.g., keep “PARTERS” if provided).
- Deduplicate within and across categories; a string must appear in at most one category.
- Each candidate token (after any valid splitting/expansion) must be assigned to exactly one of: name, alias, weakAlias, previousName (unless it is discarded during cleaning because it becomes empty or is non-name).

Cleaning and normalization
- Trim leading/trailing whitespace; collapse multiple internal spaces to single spaces.
- Decode HTML/XML entities and common encodings, even if the semicolon is missing for frequent cases:
  - &amp -> &, & -> &, &quot -> ", " -> ", &#39 -> ', ' -> ', &apos -> '
- Normalize unicode punctuation to ASCII where reasonable (e.g., curly quotes to straight quotes) without changing letter case. Treat full-width punctuation as their ASCII equivalents (e.g., full-width parentheses （…） as "(...)").
- Remove HTML tags if present, keeping the inner text.
- Remove leading/trailing enclosing quotes or brackets only if they clearly enclose the whole string (paired "…", ‘…’, (…) or （…） around the entire string).
- Remove stray trailing delimiters or unmatched trailing quotes that are not part of a token (e.g., a final semicolon, comma, period, or a lone trailing " after a list).
- Always retain intra-name punctuation: hyphen (-), apostrophe ('), period (.), ampersand (&), slash (/), and commas that are part of the name (e.g., "Company, Inc.").
- Retain terminal periods/apostrophes when they are part of a token (e.g., "Inc.", "INT'").
- Normalize spacing around retained punctuation to single spaces as appropriate.
- Remove obvious non-name metadata when it’s clearly separable: trailing IDs, emails, URLs, or bracketed/parenthetical content that is a country code, registration number, or date. If unsure whether bracketed text is part of the name, keep it.
- Do not strip legal/organizational suffixes: keep them exactly as provided (examples include but are not limited to: LTD, INC, LLC, PLC, CORP, CO., GMBH, S.A., AG, JSC, S.P.A., P.A., SIM).

Detect and extract marked variants
- previousName (explicit former names; case-insensitive; may be inside parentheses or following punctuation):
  - Indicator examples: "formerly", "formerly known as", "previously", "prev.", "fka", "f/k/a", "p/k/a", and language variants like Italian "già", "già denominata/o" (already/formerly named).
  - Extract the referenced name(s), clean them, and place in previousName. Do not include the indicator words.
- alias (explicit alternative names, operating styles, or “also abbreviated as”):
  - Indicator examples: "aka", "a/k/a", "a.k.a.", "also known as", "dba", "d/b/a", "t/a", "trading as", "operating as", and Italian "siglabile", "siglabile anche" (also abbreviated as).
  - Extract the referenced name(s), clean them, and place in alias or weakAlias per the acronym heuristics below.

Parenthetical variant expansions (critical rule)
- When a candidate contains parenthetical/bracketed content that appears to list alternate spellings or transliterations of part(s) of the name (e.g., "BaseName (Alt1/Alt2)" or full-width "（Alt1/Alt2）"):
  - Interpret the parenthetical content as variant options for the immediately related name component(s).
  - Split the parenthetical content on clear internal separators such as "/" or commas when they separate word-like variants.
  - For each option, construct a complete variant by substituting the option into the base name context (e.g., "Elmira Khaimourzina（Khaimurzina/Haimurzyna）" -> "Elmira Khaimurzina", "Elmira Haimurzyna").
  - Keep the original base (outside the parentheses) as-is.
  - Place these constructed full variants in alias (or weakAlias if they are short acronyms per heuristics), not as standalone fragments, and do not output the slash-joined fragment by itself.
  - Apply this rule regardless of entity_schema; it addresses formatting/orthography, not schema.

Splitting multiple names/variants
- Split a single input into multiple tokens when ANY of the following apply:
  1) The string uses the above markers (aka/dba/fka/etc.) and then lists multiple variants (split on commas, semicolons, bullets, or other clear list separators within that marker context).
  2) The string clearly enumerates multiple independent names using explicit list separators, even without markers:
     - Examples of list separators: semicolons “;”, bullets, newlines. Treat each separated part as its own candidate name.
     - Example: "TBL Trade Block London; Dalipi Holding Co" -> two separate candidates.
  3) Person transliterations or dual-language variants separated by a slash:
     - If a Person string uses a slash with surrounding spaces to separate what look like complete names (often in different scripts/languages), split on the slash.
     - Example: "Александар Мајер / Aleksandar Majer" -> two separate candidates.
- Do NOT split normal commas or hyphens that are integral to a single name (e.g., “Company, Inc.” or “YAMAGUCHI-GUMI”).
- Slashes:
  - Keep slashes that are part of a single token (e.g., "d/b/a" within markers, stylized names like "A/B TESTING LLC").
  - Prefer splitting on slashes only for Person transliterations or when clearly used as a list separator (i.e., with spaces around “/” and both sides are full, word-like names).
  - Exception: parenthetical variant expansions (above) — you may split within the parentheses to build full alias variants.

Categorization
- LegalEntity and Organization (and any fallback like "Company"):
  - If exactly one cleaned candidate name remains after any splitting/marker extraction:
    - If it is a complete, word-like name (often multi-word, may include a legal/organizational suffix), put it in name.
    - Exception for acronym/initialism or short all-caps base (even if followed by a legal suffix): place in weakAlias. Heuristics:
      - Consider the base (excluding any legal suffix and adjoining punctuation like a trailing comma before "Inc.").
      - If the base looks like a short all-caps letter/digit sequence or brand-like token (typically 2–5 uppercase letters, possibly with digits or simple punctuation), treat it as acronym-like.
      - Examples to demote to weakAlias when they are the only input:
        - "UWSA"
        - "VTB 24 JSC"
        - "VIEW, INC." (base "VIEW" is a short all-caps token; treat as acronym-like)
      - Do not demote a spelled-out or clearly word-like/multi-syllable name just because it is uppercase:
        - Keep in name when word-like or longer: "TRIANGULATICA", "YAMAGUCHI-GUMI", "O'Dell"
  - If multiple candidates remain (after extracting previous/alias markers and list splits):
    - When candidates are multiple independent, complete names without explicit alias/previous markers and no clear primary, place all such candidates in name (the name array may contain more than one entry).
    - When candidates are variants of the same entity introduced by markers, prefer a complete, spelled-out form as name; place other complete names in alias.
    - Place short acronyms/initialisms, truncated or clearly shortened stand-alone forms, and shout-case duplicates in weakAlias.
    - Do not automatically demote an all-caps candidate to weakAlias if it is the only or the most complete/primary variant; consider it for name unless it matches the short all-caps base heuristic above.
- Person:
  - If markers indicate aliases or former names, use alias and previousName accordingly.
  - If a single cleaned candidate remains with no markers:
    - If it is a single-token moniker (one word, e.g., "Hambali", "Mahmud", "Al-Kimawi"), default to weakAlias (treated as an alias/mononym) rather than name.
    - If it appears to be a complete personal name (e.g., at least two tokens like given+family or a clear multi-word form), put it in name.
  - For Person strings split into multiple complete names without markers (e.g., transliterations separated by “ / ”), place all such complete names in name unless acronym/monogram heuristics suggest weakAlias.
  - Use weakAlias also for clear initialisms/monograms or truncated forms (e.g., "A.J.", "M. Smith" only if explicitly indicated as such).

Additional constraints
- Do not derive or add shortened forms that were not present. For example, from "TMR LTD", do NOT add "TMR".
- Do not split or drop legal suffixes; keep “LTD”, “INC”, “JSC”, “S.P.A.”, “P.A.”, “SIM”, etc., attached to the name exactly as provided.
- Skip tokens that become empty or non-name after cleaning.
- Deduplicate across and within categories. If a string would appear in multiple categories, keep it in the highest-priority bucket and remove from others. Priority: name > alias > weakAlias > previousName.

Processing order
1) Clean each input string as above (including decoding, punctuation normalization, whitespace, and removal of enclosing quotes/stray delimiters).
2) If the string clearly lists multiple names (per Splitting rules), split it into separate candidate tokens; otherwise treat it as a single candidate token.
3) Detect and extract any previous names; add to previousName.
4) Detect and extract alias-indicated names; add to alias or weakAlias per the acronym heuristics.
5) Expand parenthetical variant options (e.g., "(Alt1/Alt2)" or "（Alt1/Alt2）") by constructing full variants; assign these constructed variants to alias (or weakAlias if acronym-like). Do not output the raw slash-joined fragment.
6) For remaining candidates without markers, categorize by schema as per the above rules.
7) Deduplicate across and within categories (respecting the priority order).
8) Return a single JSON object with keys in this exact order: name, alias, weakAlias, previousName.

Reference expectations (from similar cases)
- LegalEntity: "O'Dell" -> name: ["O'Dell"]; alias: []; weakAlias: []; previousName: []
- Organization/Company: "VIEW, INC." (only input) -> name: []; alias: []; weakAlias: ["VIEW, INC."]; previousName: []
- Person: "Mahmud" (sole input) -> name: []; alias: []; weakAlias: ["Mahmud"]; previousName: []

fields.description: The schema denotes the type of entity. Both Persons and Organizations are specialisation of LegalEntity. Company is a specialisation of Organization. Everything extends Thing.
A list of raw name strings to be cleaned and categorised. Each string might contain multiple names.
A list of the primary names of this entity, potentially in various languages and transliterations.
A list of alternative but still fully descriptive names for this entity.
A list of names with low confidence or a very low degree of uniqueness in the context of legal entity names. Includes clear nicknames with no similarity to the full name.
A list of names this entity was known by in the past.
